title: The Secretary

jokes.text: What's supposed to be so attractive about fur? Why does a man wanna see a woman in fur? Men want women with shaved legs, shaved armpits, plucked eyebrows. Then before we go out, we dress them up like a bear?
jokes.setup: Jerry questions the appeal of fur clothing on women
jokes.punchline: Contrasting modern beauty standards (hairless) with dressing women like hairy bears
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational comedy with strong internal contradiction. Well-structured buildup to the bear comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why do women wear fur coats? So they look like beautiful animals. But they're already taking all the hair off their bodies. They're removing every hair from their body with wax and razors and chemicals. So why would you then wrap yourself in fur? You're like a hairless cat wearing a dead beaver.

jokes.text: To me, the only reason to wear fur would be if you were trying to sneak up on another animal. You ever see those tribal hunters wearing the fur with the other animal's head on top of their head?
jokes.setup: Jerry proposes the only logical use for fur clothing
jokes.punchline: Hunting disguise with animal head
jokes.explanation: Solid continuation of the fur premise but less punchy than the opener

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The only logical use for fur clothing is as a hunting disguise. You put on a bear suit, you walk up to another bear, the bear thinks you're a bear. But then you gotta commit. You can't just stand there. You gotta do bear things. You gotta go find some honey, climb a tree, scratch your back on a tree. The bear's watching you going, 'That's not how we do it.' And if you mess up even once, that bear's gonna know you're not a bear. He's gonna attack you. And he's gonna rip your head clean off your body.

jokes.text: I'm sure there's a moose looking at that going, 'Yeah. That looks good. Yeah. I'm gonna turn my back on this goofball with the extra head because there's nothing fishy there. I'll just keep drinking from the stream. I've seen a lot of two-headed tigers with knees.'
jokes.setup: Jerry imagines a moose's perspective on hunters in disguise
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic moose commentary ending with 'two-headed tigers with knees'
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation with quotable ending. The moose's sarcastic voice and 'two-headed tigers with knees' is memorably bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A moose sees a hunter in camouflage and thinks, 'Oh look, a bush with a gun. How clever. I've never seen that before.' And then when there's two hunters together, the moose is like, 'Oh wow, two-headed tigers with knees. That's new.'

jokes.text: You were taking messages for your mother. And now someone's gonna be taking messages for me. From your mother.
jokes.setup: George is excited about getting a secretary
jokes.punchline: Jerry points out the circular irony of the situation
jokes.explanation: Tight writing with excellent economy. Perfect character-specific observation about George's situation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm getting a secretary! Finally, someone to help me organize my work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're hiring someone to do your job for you?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, she's just going to help me with the administrative stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But George, you don't do anything. You're going to need a secretary for your secretary.

jokes.text: Sure, I could go the tomato route... but I've given this a lot of thought, Jerry. All that frustration, I'll never get any work done. So I'm doing a complete 360.
jokes.setup: George explains his hiring strategy using 'tomato route' euphemism
jokes.punchline: His supposedly thoughtful decision to do a 'complete 360'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George's pseudo-intellectual rationalization and malapropism setup

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I think I'm gonna try the opposite. I used to do the opposite of every instinct I had.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: If every instinct you have is wrong, then the opposite would have to be right.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going in.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're really gonna do this?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm gonna do it. If I don't do this now, I'm gonna regret it for the rest of my life.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hello, my name is George. I'm unemployed and I live with my parents.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: My God, that's gold, Jerry! Gold!

jokes.text: That's a 180, George. Whatever.
jokes.setup: George's malapropism about doing a '360'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's correction followed by George's dismissive 'Whatever'
jokes.explanation: Simple but perfectly executed character moment. George's 'Whatever' dismissal is very him.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm doing a complete 360.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A 360? Don't you mean a 180?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Whatever.

jokes.text: Yeah. That's why I said 'hi.'
jokes.setup: Willie introduces Jerry to his wife Donna, whom Jerry already knows
jokes.punchline: Jerry's deadpan explanation of why he already greeted her
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry deadpan with perfect economy. Highlights the awkwardness of the unnecessary introduction.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, we've met before.

jokes.text: It really accentuates my bust line.
jokes.setup: Casual conversation about Donna's houndstooth jacket
jokes.punchline: Donna's unexpectedly intimate comment about her bust line
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor from oversharing. Sets up Jerry's discomfort but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I got this jacket at a great price. The houndstooth pattern really flatters me.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: You know, the pattern is so busy up top that nobody notices my chest isn't as big as it used to be.

jokes.text: Well, Miss Coggins, you're obviously qualified for the job. You have all the necessary skills and experience... but you're extremely attractive. You're gorgeous. I'm looking at you, I can't even remember my name.
jokes.setup: George interviews an attractive secretary candidate
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate confession about forgetting his name
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe comedy that's very George. The escalating inappropriateness builds well.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I... I completely forgot your name.

dialogue.speaker: Secretary
dialogue.line: It's on my resume.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Right, of course. I just... sometimes when I'm nervous, I blank on things. Especially with someone as attractive as you.

jokes.text: So I'm afraid this is not gonna work out. Thanks for coming. You're luscious. You're ravishing. I would give up red meat just to get a glimpse of you in a bra.
jokes.setup: George trying to professionally reject the candidate
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate escalation ending with the bra comment
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and perfectly George. The red meat/bra line is memorably absurd and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I appreciate you coming in, but I don't think you're right for the job.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not you, it's... well, actually it is you. Your qualifications just aren't there.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And frankly, your whole presentation is off. The way you're sitting, the way you're looking at me...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And that blouse! What are you trying to prove with that blouse? And I'm guessing the bra underneath isn't helping matters!

jokes.text: So you're saying, store, hotsy-totsy, Home, hotsy-notsy.
jokes.setup: Elaine explains how the dress looked great in store but terrible at home
jokes.punchline: Jerry's rhyming summary with 'hotsy-totsy/hotsy-notsy'
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay that's very Jerry. 'Hotsy-notsy' is a memorable coined phrase that captures the concept perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is, it looked hotsy-totsy in the store, but when you got it home, it was hotsy-notsy.

jokes.text: Don't know why I didn't have one before. Because you didn't have a job? Perhaps.
jokes.setup: George wonders why he never had a secretary before
jokes.punchline: Jerry's obvious answer followed by George's casual 'Perhaps'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Jerry's straightforward logic vs George's pretentious 'Perhaps' dismissal.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because no one would want to work for you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Perhaps.

jokes.text: A lesser man would've crumbled. They would've gone for the dish and the sure-fire sexual harassment suit.
jokes.setup: George praising himself for hiring the efficient Ada
jokes.punchline: His self-congratulation for avoiding 'the dish' and harassment suits
jokes.explanation: Pure George delusion. His self-congratulation for basic professional behavior is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I hired Ada. She's very efficient. She does all the work, I take all the credit.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't have to do any work, I don't have to do the dishes, I don't have to worry about sexual harassment suits.

jokes.text: You got no waist in that thing. And your arms look like something hanging in a kosher deli.
jokes.setup: Elaine modeling the dress that looked good in the store
jokes.punchline: Jerry's brutal comparison to hanging deli meat
jokes.explanation: Vivid and harsh visual comparison. The kosher deli image is both specific and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is that? You look like a side of beef hanging in a butcher shop.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm serious, you look like prosciutto.

jokes.text: This woman who was just walking by... said I looked like Demi Moore in Indecent Proposal. How fast was she walking?
jokes.setup: Elaine recounts the compliment she received at Barneys
jokes.punchline: Jerry's implication that the woman was rushing and didn't get a good look
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and punchline structure. Jerry's question implies the compliment was meaningless due to speed.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This woman at Barneys complimented my outfit. She said, 'That's a great outfit.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She was probably just rushing to the register. She didn't get a good look at you.

jokes.text: 'Demi'? I thought it was 'Demi.' No, I think it's 'Demi.' Really? I never heard of a 'semi' tractor-trailer.
jokes.setup: Discussion about pronunciation of Demi Moore's name
jokes.punchline: George's illogical connection to semi-trucks
jokes.explanation: Typical George non-sequitur logic. The connection makes no sense but is delivered with confidence.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's Demi, like a demi-god. Half god, half human.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Right.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So Demi Moore is half Moore.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Half Moore?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, like a semi-truck. Semi Moore!

jokes.text: Skinny mirrors. Barneys has skinny mirrors. They make you look, like, 10 pounds lighter.
jokes.setup: Jerry solves the mystery of why Elaine looked better at Barneys
jokes.punchline: The concept of deceptive 'skinny mirrors'
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational comedy that introduces a relatable concept. 'Skinny mirrors' became a cultural reference.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what it is? Skinny mirrors. That's what they have at Barneys. They make you look thinner. It's a conspiracy. They want you to buy the clothes.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Skinny mirrors?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, they stretch you vertically and compress you horizontally. It's like a fun house mirror, but for shopping. You look great, you buy the outfit, you get home, and boom — you're back to normal size.

jokes.text: Moisturizer? That's girl stuff. No. Look, I'll tell you what.
jokes.setup: Jerry reacts to Kramer asking him to buy moisturizer
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate defensiveness about 'girl stuff' then quick reversal
jokes.explanation: Standard masculine insecurity joke. Jerry's quick backtrack is mildly amusing but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm not buying moisturizer for you!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because that's girl stuff!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So? So I don't buy girl stuff. I'm a man.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Moisturizer is not girl stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're right, you're right. I use it myself. I have very dry skin. My dermatologist recommended it. I'll pick some up for you.

jokes.text: Ada, you're a wonder. Okay. Now, I projected those figures for you regarding the switch... to canola oil for stadium popcorn. And surprisingly, it'll only cost half a cent more per bag... so it is definitely doable.
jokes.setup: George praising his secretary's efficiency
jokes.punchline: Ada's detailed analysis of stadium popcorn oil costs
jokes.explanation: Absurd workplace detail that's very Seinfeld. The mundane precision of canola oil analysis is amusing.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Ada, you're incredibly efficient. You handle everything so well.

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: Well, I do my best. I keep detailed records of everything - expenses, supplies, even the cost per ounce of the oil they use for the popcorn at the stadium.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The stadium popcorn oil?

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: Yes. Did you know it's actually 40% more expensive than regular cooking oil? And they go through approximately 200 gallons per game. That's roughly $8,000 a season just in popcorn oil costs.

jokes.text: Do you... Do you know what I'm... thinking about now? Yes. I think I do. Is it... doable? It's definitely doable.
jokes.setup: George's hesitant proposition to his secretary
jokes.punchline: Ada's professional language ('doable') applied to sexual proposition
jokes.explanation: Good callback humor using Ada's professional terminology. The awkward buildup makes the payoff work.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So, um, Ada... I was wondering if maybe you'd like to, uh, go out sometime. Just the two of us.

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: Well, that might be doable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Doable? What do you mean 'doable'? Either you want to or you don't.

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: I mean it's something I could work into my schedule.

jokes.text: He's got the kavorca.
jokes.setup: Jerry mentions meeting Uma Thurman
jokes.punchline: Kramer's explanation using his made-up term 'kavorca'
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer neologism. 'Kavorca' became a memorable Seinfeld term for animal magnetism.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Uma Thurman? That's a kavorca!

jokes.text: I think he was wearing my houndstooth jacket. What would he be doing wearing your jacket? It looked just like the jacket I brought in to be dry-cleaned.
jokes.setup: Jerry spots Willie wearing what looks like his jacket
jokes.punchline: The implication that his dry cleaner is wearing his clothes
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the dry cleaner subplot. Relatable paranoia about service providers.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Is that my jacket?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It could be.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'it could be'? That's my jacket!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, maybe your dry cleaner's been wearing it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My dry cleaner? He's wearing my clothes?

jokes.text: No, no, no. A better way to reach the bra... would be to undo the jacket then go around the back of the shirt. Ada, you're incredible.
jokes.setup: Implied scene of George and Ada in compromising position
jokes.punchline: George treating sex like a work efficiency problem with Ada's 'incredible' response
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. George applying workplace efficiency to intimacy is perfectly him.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: We need to optimize this. I've been thinking we could save time if we just... you know, streamline the whole thing. Maybe a checklist?

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: A checklist?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's more efficient. In, out, done. No wasted motion.

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: That's the most romantic thing anyone's ever said to me.

jokes.text: I'm giving you a raise! Yes!
jokes.setup: George and Ada in the middle of sex
jokes.punchline: George shouting about a raise at the moment of climax
jokes.explanation: Peak George moment. The workplace/sex confusion is brilliantly absurd and became iconic. Perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes! Yes! Yes! I got the raise! I got the raise!

jokes.text: So you're having sex, then all of a sudden you just blurt out: 'I'm giving you a raise'?
jokes.setup: George's confession about the raise incident
jokes.punchline: Jerry's incredulous recap highlighting the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man reaction that amplifies the previous joke's absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. You went into your boss's office, told him you deserved a raise, he said no, and then you just... left?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, yes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You didn't negotiate? You didn't follow up? You just accepted his rejection and walked out?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was flustered!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, that's not how you ask for a raise. You have to be confident. You have to make them understand your value.

jokes.text: So you're so grateful to have sex, you'll shout out anything... that comes into your head. I didn't think ahead.
jokes.setup: Jerry analyzing George's psychology
jokes.punchline: George's simple admission 'I didn't think ahead'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. George's matter-of-fact admission of his lack of forethought is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what's the problem?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I didn't think ahead.

jokes.text: Maybe you could have sex with her again and then take it back.
jokes.setup: George's dilemma about the promised raise
jokes.punchline: Jerry's logical but absurd solution
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurd logic delivered deadpan. The idea of 'taking back' a raise during sex is memorably ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't go back in there. I promised him a raise and I can't deliver.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So don't go back in there.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I have to go back in there, I work there!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No you don't. You just think you do. There's no law that says you have to show up to work.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, there are laws. Labor laws.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Those are just guidelines, George. Just don't show up tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't just not show up.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why not? You're taking a personal day.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: For how long?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Forever. You never go back.

jokes.text: How long has she been there? Three days. It's almost a week.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks about Ada's tenure
jokes.punchline: George's exaggeration of 3 days being 'almost a week'
jokes.explanation: Typical George time distortion. His tendency to exaggerate duration fits his character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So how long did Ada work there?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Three days.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Three days?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was almost a week.

jokes.text: These mirrors are skinny mirrors. This is false... reflecting. And I think that the department of... you know, whatever... would be very interested to know what's going on here.
jokes.setup: Elaine discovers the skinny mirrors at Barneys
jokes.punchline: Her indignant but confused threat to 'the department of... whatever'
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Elaine's characteristic mix of outrage and uncertainty about who to complain to.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: These are skinny mirrors! They make you look thinner!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm going to report this to the department of... whatever!

jokes.text: Wait till that Uma smells this UVA.
jokes.setup: Kramer showing off his moisturizer for his date
jokes.punchline: His confusion of UVA protection with romance appeal
jokes.explanation: Typical Kramer logic mixing up technical terms with seduction. Very character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I got this great moisturizer. It's got UVA protection.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: UVA protection? What does that have to do with a date?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: UVA, Jerry. U-V-A. The ladies love it.

jokes.text: Forty-two? That's what I am now. I've been working out. I'm huge.
jokes.setup: Bania and Kramer discussing suit sizes
jokes.punchline: Bania's delusional pride about being size 42 and claiming to be 'huge'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Bania. His delusional body image is consistently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: I'm a size 42.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A size 42? That's huge!

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: I know, I'm a big guy.

jokes.text: Surely you jest. One seventy-five? Look at the stitching. This is old-world craftsmanship. Three hundred dollars. Sold.
jokes.setup: Kramer negotiating the sale of his vintage suit
jokes.punchline: His dramatic sales pitch followed by Bania's immediate acceptance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and character work. Kramer's theatrical sales approach works perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: This suit is a classic. One owner, barely worn. Italian silk, hand-stitched. You won't find another one like it.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: I'll take it.

jokes.text: There's nothing there I haven't seen before.
jokes.setup: Bania points out they're going into the women's dressing room
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual dismissal implying extensive experience
jokes.explanation: Typical Kramer boundary-crossing with casual confidence. His matter-of-fact delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So?

jokes.text: She pretends to be a teacher. It's pathetic. What does that cost? Two and a quarter?
jokes.setup: George fabricating Ada's financial struggles to Steinbrenner
jokes.punchline: His casual knowledge of high school cafeteria prices revealing his own experience
jokes.explanation: Great character moment. George's specific knowledge of cafeteria prices suggests his own experience with the scheme.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She's really struggling financially. I mean, she can barely afford to eat. Yesterday she had to make lunch at home instead of buying it at the cafeteria.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: A cafeteria lunch? How much does that cost?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh, I don't know, three fifty, four bucks...

jokes.text: She'd be better off making a sandwich and bringing it.
jokes.setup: George's elaborate sob story about Ada's financial struggles
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner's practical but tone-deaf response about sandwiches
jokes.explanation: Good Steinbrenner character moment. His complete missing of the point is very him.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Mr. Steinbrenner, my mother's friend Ada, she's going through terrible financial hardship. Her electricity's been shut off, she can barely afford to eat.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: That's a shame, Costanza. You know what I do when money's tight? I make sandwiches at home instead of eating out. Saves a fortune.

jokes.text: Specifically, 9:30 shows. Seen any good 9:30 shows at the Paragon, Willie?
jokes.setup: Jerry casually asking Willie about movies
jokes.punchline: His specific mention of the 9:30 show time, clearly confronting him
jokes.explanation: Good confrontational buildup. Jerry's passive-aggressive approach is very him.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So Willie, you go to the movies a lot?

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I love movies.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever go to the 9:30 show?

jokes.text: Jerry, that's a breach of the dry cleaners' code. You need a code to tell you not to wear people's clothes?
jokes.setup: Willie invokes a 'dry cleaners' code'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's logical response questioning the need for such a code
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist concept with Jerry's perfect logical response. The idea of a 'dry cleaners' code' is memorably ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: We have a code at the dry cleaners.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A code? What code? It's a dry cleaners, not a speakeasy.

jokes.text: Oh, I know, but 25,000. So you got a $25,000-a-year raise? Yes. You're making more than I am.
jokes.setup: George learns the size of Ada's raise
jokes.punchline: The revelation that his secretary now makes more than he does
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and payoff. The absurd consequences of George's sex-fueled promise create perfect irony.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, your secretary makes more than you?

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: She got a raise.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How much more?

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: Fifteen thousand dollars more a year.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Fifteen thousand?! Your secretary makes fifteen thousand dollars more than you do?

dialogue.speaker: Ada
dialogue.line: She's very efficient.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: She's efficient? I'm a real estate developer! I went to college!

jokes.text: Oh, this is insanity. I'm not this hip-y.
jokes.setup: Elaine looking at herself in normal mirrors
jokes.punchline: Her distressed realization about her actual hip size using 'hip-y'
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the mirror subplot. Elaine's invented adjective 'hip-y' is characteristically creative.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God. I'm hip-y.

jokes.text: You sold your pants to Bania? Let me in. Why'd you sell your pants to Bania? I had Uma Thurman's number written on that stub.
jokes.setup: Jerry discovers Kramer sold his clothes to Bania
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Uma's number was on the dry cleaning stub
jokes.explanation: Good escalation connecting multiple plot threads. The convergence of the Uma storyline with the pants sale is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, you sold my clothes to Bania?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: He needed them!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My clothes? You can't just sell my clothes!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I found Uma Thurman's number on the dry cleaning stub in the pocket.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was right there the whole time. You had Uma Thurman's number and you didn't even know it.

jokes.text: How'd you expect to get out of here? Well, I didn't think ahead.
jokes.setup: Jerry questions Kramer's logic about selling his clothes
jokes.punchline: Kramer's admission echoing George's earlier line
jokes.explanation: Nice callback connecting George and Kramer's similar lack of foresight. The repetition creates unity.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're selling all your clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm starting over. New life, new wardrobe.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But Kramer, you can't just sell your clothes and expect everything to change.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Why not? It's worked for me before.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: When has it ever worked?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well... it hasn't. But I'm not giving up.

jokes.text: Well, I suppose that salt stain came from all the snow in the store.
jokes.setup: Elaine tries to return the dress claiming she didn't wear it outside
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic response about snow in the store explaining the salt stain
jokes.explanation: Sharp sarcastic comeback that catches Elaine in her lie. Good retail confrontation humor.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I didn't wear it outside.

dialogue.speaker: Salesperson
dialogue.line: Oh, so it snowed in here?

jokes.text: Your nancy-boy cream leaked all over the pockets. Suit's ruined.
jokes.setup: Bania complaining about the moisturizer damage
jokes.punchline: His use of 'nancy-boy cream' as an insult
jokes.explanation: Bania's casual homophobia is period-appropriate but dated. More cringe than clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: This moisturizer, it's ruined me! My face is all soft now. The guys at work are saying I look like I've been taking care of myself.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: Taking care of myself? What am I, some kind of nancy-boy? Nancy-boy cream!

jokes.text: You give me the ticket... and I will take you out for a nice dinner. Can we go back to Mendy's? You wanna go to Mendy's, I'll take you to Mendy's. Twice. I wanna go twice.
jokes.setup: Jerry negotiating with Bania for the ticket
jokes.punchline: Bania's escalating demands ending with wanting to go twice
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Bania's characteristic pushiness. His 'twice' demand is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you'll come to the movie?

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know. What kind of movie is it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's a good movie. You'll like it.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you gotta throw in dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Dinner? I'm already giving you the ticket.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm hungry. And I want it to be a nice place.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Fine, fine. Dinner at a nice place.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: And I want you to pay for parking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Parking? What else?

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: And I want to go twice.

jokes.text: Sometimes I can't even make up my mind. A lot of times, I'll mix the two together. Make a vanilla-fudge.
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner's tangent about cupcake preferences
jokes.punchline: His creation of 'vanilla-fudge' combination
jokes.explanation: Pure Steinbrenner rambling. The character work is perfect but the joke itself isn't particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about cupcakes. Vanilla or chocolate, that's what everybody says. Vanilla or chocolate. But what if... what if you combined them? Vanilla on the outside, fudge on the inside. You'd have the best of both worlds.

jokes.text: The moisturizer smudged out the phone number. The dry-cleaning number's gone too. It must have been the botanical extracts.
jokes.setup: The ticket is retrieved but damaged
jokes.punchline: Kramer's pseudo-scientific explanation for the moisturizer damage
jokes.explanation: Good convergence of plot elements with typical Kramer pseudo-expertise about 'botanical extracts.'

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: The moisturizer, it's breaking down the cellular walls. See, the skin is like a big sponge, and the moisture, it's seeping in, and it's causing the fibers to separate.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not what's happening.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, I'm telling you, it's a chemical reaction. The cream is too potent. You gotta dilute it with water.

jokes.text: Hey, isn't that my mother's fur coat? No, it's not. It is. Give me that back. What are you talking about? You out of your mind?
jokes.setup: Jerry spots Willie wearing his mother's fur coat
jokes.punchline: The escalating confrontation and Willie's denials
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and payoff for the dry cleaner subplot. Physical confrontation adds energy.

jokes.text: I told you Mendy's had the best pea soup. The best, Jerry. The best.
jokes.setup: Bania and Jerry at dinner
jokes.punchline: Bania's enthusiastic repetition of 'the best' about pea soup
jokes.explanation: Pure Bania character work. His enthusiastic repetition is very him but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: This is the best pea soup I've ever had.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: The best! The best!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's pea soup, Bob.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: The best pea soup. The best!

jokes.text: It's a gamble. I get it.
jokes.setup: Jerry's complex analysis of restaurant choice dilemma
jokes.punchline: Bania's oversimplified understanding reducing it to 'a gamble'
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast. Bania's simple understanding of Jerry's overthinking is perfectly him.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're saying we go to Monk's, we're committing to a certain type of meal. We're locked in. But if we go to that new place, we're taking a chance. We could have an amazing meal, or it could be a disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: So it's a gamble.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not a gamble, it's a calculated risk based on multiple variables including ambiance, menu diversity, and reputation.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: A gamble.

jokes.text: Some woman named Uma. I got her number off that ticket before it was smudged. Hope she's good-looking.
jokes.setup: Jerry mentions his date with Uma
jokes.punchline: Bania's casual hope that Uma Thurman is 'good-looking'
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment with Bania not recognizing one of the most famous actresses. Nice callback closure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have a date with Uma.

dialogue.speaker: Bania
dialogue.line: Uma Thurman? Well, I hope she's good-looking.

jokes.text: I don't wanna hear about the specials. I don't wanna know about the specials, I'm sick of the specials, I hate them. My feeling is, if the specials were so special, they'd be on the menu.
jokes.setup: Jerry's standup about restaurant specials
jokes.punchline: His logic that if specials were special, they'd be on the menu
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry observational comedy with solid logic. The escalating frustration builds to a quotable insight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice how restaurants have these things called 'specials'? If it's so special, why isn't it on the menu? You know what that tells me? It's the stuff they couldn't sell. It's a way of getting rid of food. 'We got some fish here, been sitting around since Tuesday. Let's make it a special.' Nobody wants it at regular price, so we knock a buck fifty off and suddenly it's special.

jokes.text: The veal is lightly slapped... and then sequestered in a one-bedroom suite... with a white-wine intravenous.
jokes.setup: Jerry mocking overly creative menu descriptions
jokes.punchline: His absurd parody using hotel and medical terminology
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation. The image of veal being 'sequestered' with an 'intravenous' is memorably ridiculous and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They describe everything like it's the most important thing you've ever eaten. 'Our sea urchin is delicately poached in a reduction of saffron and tears of a mermaid.' It's a sea urchin! Just tell me if it's good!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I went to a restaurant the other day, they had this on the menu: 'Chicken, air-dried for 47 days in a Tibetan monastery, then slowly massaged back to life.'

tmdb_overview: Clothing problems are a hang-up; George hires a secretary.
insight: Mid-tier observational hour struggles to sustain momentum across 52 scattered jokes.
